06SM200-400.heute: Two-semester lecture on the emergence, persistence and treatment of mental disorders — psychopathology, classification models (RDoC, HiTOP), diagnostics, mechanisms of change and therapeutic approaches; written exam, mandatory confidentiality declaration.
06SM200-400.zukunft: The future module uses the mandatory confidentiality declaration as its didactic anchor for building KI5 Taking responsibility + Fu3 at level 2: students learn to classify sensitive clinical information and never enter it into non-approved tools (APA, 2023; Bass et al., 2025). In parallel, clinical judgement (Fa1, Fa8, Fa5) is sharpened on AI-generated case vignettes: an AI proposes a preliminary diagnosis, and students test it against classification criteria and the primary literature (KI6 Evaluating + Fu1, KI3 Collaborating + Fu3; Miao et al., 2024). Deskilling protection stays central: the written exam secures core clinical and ethical knowledge AI-free.
lernziele: Students classify clinical case information by sensitivity and justify which data must never enter non-approved tools.
Students test an AI-generated preliminary diagnosis against classification systems (e.g. RDoC, HiTOP) and the textbook.
Students explain central disorder models and mechanisms of change without AI support.
Students derive concrete rules for AI use in clinical contexts from the confidentiality declaration.
06SM200-400.ki: Category [A]+[B] — the written exam secures core clinical and ethical knowledge AI-free [A]; accompanying declared AI case-vignette tasks with disclosure [B]. Activating elements: AI role-play (simulated client) and an AI-critique exercise (preliminary diagnosis vs. classification criteria). Every AI use is declared via the disclosure form.

quest.text: Take a short anonymised case vignette and have an AI formulate a preliminary diagnosis. Check the diagnosis against your textbook's classification criteria, and mark every piece of information in the vignette red, amber or green: never, only anonymised, or safe to enter into a non-approved tool.

06SM200-401.heute: Lecture presenting biopsychological diagnostic and intervention methods for mental and physical illness across the lifespan, based on the bio-psycho-social model and illustrated with case examples; written exam.
06SM200-401.zukunft: The future module deepens Fa1 and Fa8 (level 2) on biopsychological mechanisms and markers and uses them as concrete practice material for KI4 Understanding + Fu2: students learn how AI-supported models propose biomarker–mechanism links and why such claims can sound convincing without being substantiated (Bass et al., 2025). Via KI6 Evaluating + Fu1 they test these claims against the primary literature instead of adopting them (Miao et al., 2024). The [A] exam secures the biopsychological foundations; newskilling lies in critically appraising AI claims about markers and mechanisms.
lernziele: Students explain central biopsychological markers and mechanisms of mental and physical illness without AI.
Students identify a plausible-sounding but unsubstantiated claim in an AI explanation of a biomarker.
Students test an AI-generated mechanism hypothesis against a primary source and appraise its evidence.
06SM200-401.ki: Category [A] — the written exam secures core biopsychological knowledge AI-free; accompanied by formative [B] exercises. Activating element: live check («Is what the AI says about this biomarker correct?»). AI claims are verified against the primary literature; no mandatory tools with external logins.

quest.text: Have an AI explain through which biological marker (e.g. cortisol reactivity) a psychological intervention works. Compare the explanation with your lecture materials and mark exactly one sentence that looks like a hallucination — and note how you would test it against a primary source.

06SM200-402.heute: Computerised self-study programme (PTO) over two semesters covering central disorder patterns; assessment consists of six exercises, each with open questions and a case analysis including a preliminary diagnosis, minimum grade 4 each, punctual submission required.
06SM200-402.zukunft: As an asynchronous take-home format, the PTO is the module group's most AI-vulnerable assessment — so the future module adds an explicit verification component that secures Fu2 («without AI») and KI5 Taking responsibility at level 2 (Perkins et al., 2025). Students still build disorder knowledge themselves (Fa1, Fa8, Fa10); a short randomised AI-free checkpoint per curriculum verifies that case analysis and preliminary diagnosis can be explained independently (QAA, 2023). Via KI6 Evaluating, students also critically test an AI answer to a case vignette against the classification.
lernziele: Students recognise and describe central psychopathological disorder patterns in clinical settings.
Students make and justify a preliminary diagnosis independently, without AI, in the verification component.
Students test an AI-generated case analysis against classification criteria and name its factual errors.
Students declare every AI use in the PTO transparently via the disclosure form.
06SM200-402.ki: Category [A]+[B] — the six exercises remain AI-usable with declaration [B], complemented by a randomised AI-free verification checkpoint [A] per curriculum. Activating elements: prompt-and-disclosure exercise and AI critique of a case vignette. Verification duty: the preliminary diagnosis must be explained in person at the checkpoint.

quest.text: Work on an open PTO question for five minutes entirely without AI and note your preliminary diagnosis. Then obtain an AI answer, compare the two, and justify in three sentences where the AI misses the case.

06SM200-403.heute: Lecture on an interpersonal understanding of mental disorders — the role of relationships in aetiology, course and relapse, the «we-disease» concept, family risk and resilience factors, preventive and therapeutic interventions; written exam.
06SM200-403.zukunft: The future module anchors Fa1 and strengthens Fa9 Diversity & context (level 2) by putting the social and family context of mental disorders centre stage (DGPs, 2014). As AI competences, KI6 Evaluating + Fu1 and KI3 Collaborating + Fu3 are built up: students test AI-generated summaries of risk factors and «we-disease» against lecture content and evidence, and systematically add the context and diversity aspects that generic AI answers tend to miss (Bass et al., 2025; Miao et al., 2024).
lernziele: Students explain the four central family risk factors and the «we-disease» concept without AI.
Students check an AI summary of relationship factors against the lecture and add missing context.
Students judge where a generic AI answer ignores diversity and family context.
06SM200-403.ki: Category [A] — the written exam secures the interpersonal understanding of disorders AI-free; accompanied by formative [B] exercises. Activating element: AI-critique exercise (AI summary vs. lecture evidence). AI use is declared; every adopted claim is verified against the evidence.

quest.text: Have an AI list the four most important family risk factors for childhood disorders. Check the list against your lecture materials and add one diversity or context aspect the AI answer overlooked.

06SM200-500.heute: English-language lecture with tutorial: refresher on multiple regression, then multilevel (mixed) models with a focus on longitudinal data and structural equation models (SEM) for measuring change, practised hands-on on students' own laptops. Assessment: written exam.
06SM200-500.zukunft: Statistics 3 becomes the methodological backbone of stage 3 and the TIC pilot module (priority 1) for activating large lectures: peer instruction and live checks bring students back into active thinking. Fa3 Statistics, R & data and Fa2 are built at level 3: students not only fit multilevel and structural equation models, they defend model choice, assumptions and interpretation. KI4 Understanding (level 3) moves centre stage: explainability and model validation are trained on AI-generated model proposals — students probe where a plausible-sounding AI suggestion for SEM or multilevel modelling betrays the data structure.
lernziele: Students choose the appropriate model for hierarchical and longitudinal questions (multilevel vs. SEM) and justify the choice.
Students interpret model outputs (fixed and random effects, fit indices) correctly and explain them AI-free.
Students test AI-generated model proposals for assumptions, fit and interpretation risks and correct them.
Students debug their own R code with AI support and explain every line of the final script themselves.
06SM200-500.ki: Category [A]+[B]: the written exam stays [A] AI-free — model logic, assumptions and interpretation must be explainable without AI (deskilling protection). Exercises run [B] partially AI-integrated: AI editing and debugging of one's own R code (no code generation), probing AI model proposals; every AI step goes in the process log.

quest.text: Describe a longitudinal design to Copilot Chat (e.g. stress measured at four time points, nested in persons) and ask for an analysis model. Audit the proposal on three points: Does the model class fit the data structure? Which assumptions go unmentioned? What would you change — and why?

06SM200-501.heute: Lecture on the diagnostic process — clarifying the referral question, selecting, administering and scoring diagnostic instruments, interpretation and report writing, deciding on interventions — from foundations to quality assurance. Assessment: written exam.
06SM200-501.zukunft: Psychological assessment becomes the «master discipline» of AI evaluation in the curriculum: nowhere is the professional duty to scrutinise clearer than where algorithms judge people. The module builds Fa5 and Fa7 at level 3 — from the diagnostic process and psychometric quality criteria to report logic (DGPs, 2014) — and makes KI6 Evaluating (level 3) the third main competence: students systematically audit algorithmic diagnostic instruments for bias, fairness and measurement invariance, following the ITC/ATP guidelines for technology-based assessment and the EU AI Act's high-risk classification of algorithmic aptitude assessment.
lernziele: Students appraise diagnostic instruments — classical and algorithmic — against primary and secondary quality criteria.
Students conduct a bias/fairness audit of an algorithmic diagnostic instrument (test fairness, measurement invariance).
Students situate technology-based assessment in its regulatory frame (ITC/ATP guidelines, EU AI Act high-risk) and derive audit duties.
Students select diagnostic instruments hypothesis-driven and justify which diagnostic decisions must never be delegated to AI.
06SM200-501.ki: Category [B]/[C]: the written exam combines secured AI-free parts (quality criteria, diagnostic reasoning — deskilling protection) with [B] parts on prepared audit cases; the fully AI-integrated [C] bias audit of an algorithmic instrument is demanding newskilling, graded on steering, audit, validation, ethics and personal accountability.

quest.text: Run a mini bias audit: ask Copilot Chat to describe a fictional algorithmic selection procedure (e.g. automated CV screening with personality scores). Probe it on three questions: For which groups could the procedure judge in a systematically biased way? Which psychometric quality criterion is at risk? What would your first audit step be?

06SM200-502.heute: Lecture on concepts and determinants of health and illness, health behaviour and the central theories explaining it, stress and coping, social relationships, and health-psychological interventions for prevention and health promotion. Assessment: written exam.
06SM200-502.zukunft: Digital health becomes the connecting thread: chatbots, health apps and AI coaches are already part of health behaviour — health psychology supplies the theories and evidence standards to appraise them professionally. Fa1 and Fa8 are built at level 3: students apply models of health behaviour, stress and coping theories and intervention logic to digital interventions; Fa9 sharpens the eye for digital health inequality. KI5 Taking responsibility (level 3) is the main AI competence: students determine which health data must never enter AI systems and where professional responsibility cannot be delegated.
lernziele: Students explain central theories of health behaviour and apply them to digital interventions such as health apps.
Students appraise chatbot- and app-based health interventions by evidence base, theoretical grounding and risks.
Students judge which health data may be entered into AI systems and justify the limits of delegation.
Students take diversity and digital inequality into account when appraising digital health offerings.
06SM200-502.ki: Category [B]: the written exam contains secured AI-free parts (theories, evidence — deskilling protection) and a prepared, declared case analysis of a digital health intervention (chatbot/app) with disclosure form and source verification. Activating elements: AI role-play (Copilot agent as health coach) and AI-critique exercise.

quest.text: Evaluate a chatbot intervention: ask Copilot Chat to coach you on a behaviour-change goal (e.g. more everyday exercise) over five dialogue turns. Then assess: Which health-psychological theory do the recommendations map onto? Where is the evidence missing? Where would the bot need to hand over to professionals?

06SM200-510.heute: Lecture on decision theory and violations of rationality, advertising and nudging, game theory, cooperation and public goods, and unethical behaviour and corruption. Assessment: written exam.
06SM200-510.zukunft: The future module makes AI itself an object of study for economic and consumer psychology: recommendation algorithms, personalised advertising and dark patterns are applied choice architecture — precisely the mechanisms the module explains theoretically. Fa1 and Fa4 are built at level 3 (decision theory, rationality violations, game theory, cooperation); Fa8 connects them to marketing and policy applications. KI6 Evaluating (level 3) becomes the main AI competence: students analyse algorithmic persuasion for its psychological mechanisms and appraise «silicon samples» — LLM-simulated decisions — against empirical findings on human behaviour.
lernziele: Students explain rationality assumptions and their violations and identify them in real consumer and policy contexts.
Students analyse AI-driven choice architectures (recommender systems, personalised advertising, dark patterns) for their psychological mechanisms.
Students compare LLM-simulated decisions («silicon samples») with empirical findings on human decision-making.
Students formalise social dilemmas game-theoretically and derive conditions under which cooperation emerges.
06SM200-510.ki: Category [A]+[B]: the written exam stays [A] AI-free (decision and game theory, empirical findings — deskilling protection); accompanying [B] exercises work with AI in declared form. Activating elements: live check («Is the AI nudging you?» — analysing AI-generated advertising for persuasion techniques) and a two-stage quiz.

quest.text: Compare human and machine: have Copilot Chat play a public-goods game (four rounds, contribution 0–20 points, justification required). Compare its behaviour with empirical findings on human cooperation — such as initial contributions around 40–60 percent and their decay — and note one systematic difference.

06SM200-511.heute: Lecture on the major neuropsychological syndromes, their neurological causes, the key diagnostic procedures and basic rehabilitation approaches grounded in modern neuroscience. Assessment: written exam.
06SM200-511.zukunft: The future module brings clinical case work into the large lecture — with AI as simulation and audit object, never as decision-maker. Fa1, Fa5 and Fa8 are built at level 3: explaining syndromes, differential diagnosis, choosing procedures and rehabilitation measures case by case (DGPs, 2014). KI6 Evaluating (level 3) is sharpened on this material: students test AI-generated preliminary diagnoses and case vignettes against syndrome criteria and current literature and expose typical fallacies. KI3 Collaborating uses AI role-play: simulated patients make history-taking and findings interviews trainable at scale.
lernziele: Students explain the major neuropsychological syndromes and differentiate their neurological causes.
Students test AI-generated preliminary diagnoses for case vignettes against syndrome criteria and current literature.
Students select diagnostic procedures and basic rehabilitation measures case by case and justify the choice.
Students practise history-taking and findings interviews in AI role-play and reflect on the limits of simulated patients.
06SM200-511.ki: Category [A]+[B]: the written exam stays [A] AI-free (syndromes, differential diagnosis, procedures — deskilling protection); [B] exercises work with AI in declared form. Activating elements: AI role-play (Copilot agent as simulated patient, e.g. with neglect symptoms) and AI-critique exercises on AI case vignettes.

quest.text: Have Copilot Chat create a short fictional case vignette with visuo-spatial symptoms after a right-hemisphere stroke — including a preliminary diagnosis. Probe it: Does the AI cleanly distinguish neglect from hemianopia? Which examination would settle the differential diagnosis?

06SM200-901.heute: Master's thesis group 1 prepares the master's thesis at the supervising chair: the focus is on finding and structuring the topic with the goal of developing the thesis concept. Assessment: active participation (pass/fail).
06SM200-901.zukunft: Master's thesis group 1 becomes the workshop for the research concept and the «AI use plan»: before the thesis starts, students determine which work steps will run with AI, without AI, or fully AI-integrated — and justify this professionally (KI5 Taking responsibility, level 4; Perkins et al., 2025). In the small group they clarify human–AI roles for their project: what is delegated to AI, what remains their own work, who carries final responsibility (KI3 Collaborating + Fu3, level 4). Professionally, refining the research question, design and analysis plan is central (Fa2, level 4), carried by project planning and self-regulation (Fa10, Fu2; Ehlers et al., 2024).
lernziele: Students develop a viable concept for their master's thesis (question, design, analysis plan) and present it in the group.
Students draft an AI use plan that determines and justifies, per work step, what runs with AI, without AI, or fully AI-integrated.
Students clarify human–AI roles, verification duties and final human responsibility for their project and record them.
Students plan their project's data lifecycle and decide with justification which data must not enter AI systems.
06SM200-901.ki: Category [B]: concept development proceeds with declared AI steps; the AI use plan itself becomes the object of assessment within active participation. Activating elements: prompt-and-disclosure exercise (the AI use plan as lived disclosure) and peer instruction on concept and design decisions in the small group.

quest.text: Sketch an AI use plan for a (fictional) thesis topic: for three work steps — literature, analysis, writing — decide what runs with AI, without AI, and fully AI-integrated, and justify each decision in one sentence.

06SM200-902.heute: In research colloquium 1, the master's thesis proposal is presented to a professionally competent audience and critically discussed. Assessment: portfolio of presentation (50%) and active participation through critical discussion contributions (50%), pass/fail.
06SM200-902.zukunft: Research colloquium 1 becomes the thesis's first AI-free [A] checkpoint: the proposal is presented live and defended without AI in the room — securing that question, design and analysis plan are genuinely understood, not merely workflow-generated (Fu2, level 4; Perkins et al., 2025). Preparation may be AI-supported in declared form: in a review simulation, the AI poses critical reviewer questions and students train their answers without AI. Critical-constructive contributions on peers' proposals train scientific thinking and the scrutiny of others' AI workflow decisions (Fa4, KI6, level 4; Miao et al., 2024).
lernziele: Students present their thesis proposal to a professionally competent audience in an audience-appropriate way.
Students defend question, design and analysis plan AI-free against critical questioning.
Students formulate critical-constructive contributions on peers' project plans — including their AI decisions.
Students use AI in declared form for preparation (review simulation) and reflect on its limits.
06SM200-902.ki: Category [A] at the core: live presentation and defence are AI-free — the thesis's first secured checkpoint. Presentation preparation may be AI-supported in declared form (disclosure in the portfolio); activating elements: AI role-play as review simulation in preparation and peer instruction in the colloquium discussion.

quest.text: Have an AI generate three critical reviewer questions on your proposal (or a study idea), answer each without AI in at most two minutes — and mark the question that challenges you most.

06SM200-903.heute: Master's thesis group 2 offers ongoing supervision while the thesis is being written; relevant questions of elaboration are discussed. Assessment: active participation (pass/fail).
06SM200-903.zukunft: Master's thesis group 2 becomes the audit room for the running AI research workflow: students bring process logs, R scripts and AI outputs from their thesis and audit them together — script review, verification of statistical outputs, source-checking AI summaries against the primary literature (KI6 Evaluating, level 4; Bass et al., 2025). Those using agentic workflows (literature updates, code review, bias checks) report on configuration, constraints and failure cases and adapt them (KI2 Creating, level 4). Throughout: AI edits and reviews one's own code but does not generate it — statistical responsibility stays with the student (ETH/UZH, 2025).
lernziele: Students apply planning, analysis, interpretation and presentation of a data collection, simulation or secondary analysis to their own project.
Students audit R scripts, statistical outputs and AI summaries systematically and correct errors verifiably.
Students explain key analysis and code decisions in short AI-free checkpoints.
Students report on configuration, constraints and failure cases of their agentic workflows and adapt them.
06SM200-903.ki: Category [B]: an ongoing, partially AI-integrated workflow with process log; AI use is declared and audited in the group. Activating elements: AI-critique exercise (script and output review against original data and primary literature) and live checks on AI outputs brought to the session. Short AI-free checkpoints secure explanation ability.

quest.text: Have Copilot Chat review a short R script you wrote yourself, test two of its suggestions on the code, and document in three sentences which suggestion holds and which is faulty or superfluous.

06SM200-904.heute: In research colloquium 2, the prepared results of the master's thesis are presented and put up for discussion. Assessment: portfolio of presentation (50%) and active participation through critical discussion contributions (50%), pass/fail.
06SM200-904.zukunft: Research colloquium 2 becomes the thesis's second AI-free [A] checkpoint: results, analysis decisions and limitations are defended without AI — including which AI steps the research workflow contained and how they were verified (KI5, KI6 + Fu2, level 4; Perkins et al., 2025). Anyone who «understands» their statistics only with AI is exposed here: the colloquium secures that data analysis and critical thinking hold at level 4 without the tool (Fa3, Fa4 — deskilling protection at the decisive point). As a discussion prompt, the plenum probes AI-generated alternative interpretations of the presented findings.
lernziele: Students present the prepared results of their master's thesis and put them up for discussion.
Students defend analysis decisions, estimates and limitations AI-free and explain how AI steps in the workflow were verified.
Students appraise AI-generated alternative interpretations of presented findings for professional soundness.
Students discuss project plans and research results — their own and others' — critically and constructively.
06SM200-904.ki: Category [A] at the core: results presentation and defence are AI-free — the second secured checkpoint before submission. Activating elements: AI-critique exercise (probing AI alternative interpretations in the plenum) and peer instruction in the discussion; AI-supported presentation preparation is declared in the portfolio.

quest.text: Pick one central estimate from your (or a given) analysis and explain in five minutes, without any aids, what it means, why the method fits, and where its limits lie.

06SM200-909.heute: The master's examination is a 30-minute oral exam taken with the thesis supervisor or a person they designate. Preparation consists of working through the articles and/or textbooks published by the examiners (graded, self-study).
06SM200-909.zukunft: The master's examination becomes the «defence with/against AI» — and stays strictly [A] AI-free: in the exam room only one's own professional judgement counts, with disciplinary knowledge and scientific thinking at the programme's highest level (Fa1, Fa4, level 4; QAA, 2023). The examination scope is extended: students must be able to situate where AI carries in their research field and where it deceives — hallucinations, persuasion risks, synthetic data and algorithmic procedures (KI6 Evaluating, level 4; ETH/UZH, 2025). Preparation in self-study may be AI-supported, for instance sparring with an AI that argues counter-positions to the exam texts.
lernziele: Students reproduce methods, content and theoretical concepts of a psychological research field AI-free.
Students apply their knowledge to new research questions in the exam conversation and justify decisions.
Students situate where AI carries in their research field and where it deceives — from hallucinations to persuasion risks.
Students defend a justified position of their own against AI-generated counter-arguments and retain professional final responsibility.
06SM200-909.ki: Category [A] without exception: 30 minutes oral, own judgement only — the «defence with/against AI» examines AI competence without AI in the room. Activating elements in preparation: AI role-play (the AI argues counter-positions to the exam texts) and live checks in self-study.

quest.text: Have Copilot Chat argue a counter-position to a finding from your examination area, refute two of its arguments with evidence — and honestly name one argument that actually holds.

06SM200-BA.heute: Independent scholarly literature review following psychological standards, with an accompanying workshop; assessed on understanding, argumentative coherence, literature selection, formal criteria, correct citation and independent work; graded written thesis.
06SM200-BA.zukunft: The Bachelor's thesis is the Bachelor's «journeyman's piece», bundling Fa6, Fa4, Fa2, Fa7 and Fa10 (level 2) with a professionally declared AI workflow (Perkins et al., 2025). At the outset students draft an AI use plan determining, per work step, what is handled [A] AI-free and what [B] assisted; KI5 Taking responsibility + Fu3 and KI6 Evaluating + Fu2 secure disclosure and verification, KI1 and KI3 structure AI and peer feedback (Bass et al., 2025; Miao et al., 2024). Following the secure/open logic, the open AI-integrated thesis is not devalued but complemented by a secured defence/transfer element.
lernziele: Students draft an AI use plan marking each work step as [A] AI-free or [B] assisted.
Students document their AI use via disclosure form and process log and verify every AI-supported claim.
Students defend the central substantive and methodological decisions of their thesis AI-free.
Students write a formally correct, independently argued scholarly thesis.
06SM200-BA.ki: Category [A]+[B] following the secure/open logic — open, declared AI use with disclosure and verification [B], complemented by AI-free defence/explanation points [A]. Activating elements: prompt-and-disclosure exercise and AI critique (AI and peer feedback vs. one's own judgement). Mandatory AI use plan; no AI-generated text without declaration and verification.

quest.titel: AI Use Plan in Ten Minutes
quest.text: Break your thesis question into five work steps (e.g. search, literature mapping, argumentation, writing, citation). Mark each step [A] AI-free or [B] assisted, and for every [B] step note exactly one verification step you will use to check the AI output professionally.

06SM200-MA.heute: The master's thesis comprises conducting and documenting an empirical or methodological investigation of a scientifically relevant question using currently recognised research and analysis methods. The deliverable is a written thesis in the form of a scientific research report.
06SM200-MA.zukunft: The master's thesis becomes the credential of a professional, declared and verified AI research workflow: at level 4, students steer a complete research pipeline — literature monitoring, preregistration draft, data management, script review, visualisation and writing feedback — and personally answer for every decision (KI1 Applying; Bass et al., 2025). New are bounded agentic workflows: agents for literature updates, code review or bias checks are configured, constrained and monitored (KI2 Creating, level 4). Per the PSYCH AI Guidelines, AI-generated code remains excluded — AI serves editing and review of one's own R code, and every line stays explainable.
lernziele: Students conduct an independent empirical or methodological investigation and document it as a scientific research report.
Students steer an AI-supported research pipeline — literature monitoring, data management, script review, writing feedback — with full disclosure.
Students configure, constrain and monitor an agentic workflow (e.g. literature updates or bias checks).
Students verify AI outputs systematically against primary literature, original data and their own re-analyses, and correct them.
06SM200-MA.ki: Category [B]/[C]: the core format is [B] partially AI-integrated (process log, disclosure form, verification trail, data-protection decision); clearly designated work packages — such as agentic literature monitoring or a review simulation — run as demanding [C] newskilling, graded on steering, audit, validation, ethics and personal accountability.

quest.text: Build a mini research workflow: have an AI (e.g. Copilot Chat) summarise three current studies on your topic, verify every source for existence and correct interpretation via PsycINFO/Google Scholar, and record in a short process log which steps the AI performed — and which you verified.

06SM200-s04.heute: Elective seminar (~30 people) with one topic area per track — DeNC: «Cognitive development across the lifespan», HEA: «Clinical psychology 1», SEOP: «Social psychology 1». Students gain detailed knowledge, compare theories and practise reading and appraising journal articles.
06SM200-s04.zukunft: The module joins track-specific theory work with professionally guided scrutiny of AI systems as objects of inquiry. In the DeNC topic area this becomes central when foundation models are treated as models of cognition or development: students distinguish whether a model merely predicts behaviour or mechanistically explains it (prediction vs. explanation), testing this against developmental primary literature (Miao et al., 2024). In HEA, AI-supported case information is held against diagnostic criteria; in SEOP, social-psychological findings are defended against plausible-sounding AI claims.
lernziele: Students compare current theories of their track and appraise the strengths and weaknesses of empirical findings.
Students distinguish prediction from mechanistic explanation on a foundation-model example and substantiate the difference.
Students test AI-generated disciplinary claims against textbook and PsycINFO and correct errors verifiably.
Students declare their AI use transparently and defend their analytical decisions without AI.
06SM200-s04.ki: The three catalogue options each carry an assessment logic: written exam = [A] AI-free (explaining theories/findings oneself), written paper = [B] partially AI-integrated (with disclosure form and process log), portfolio = [C] AI-integrated (a documented model audit as the assessed deliverable — demanding newskilling).

quest.titel: Does the Model Predict or Explain?
quest.text: Have Copilot Chat explain a developmental phenomenon (e.g. object permanence or theory of mind). Mark each claim as «prediction» or «mechanistic explanation» and test one of them against a textbook. Note in two sentences where the model merely appeared to explain.

06SM200-s05.heute: Elective seminar (~30 people) with one topic area per track — DeNC: «Cognitive neuroscience / motivational development», HEA: «Prevention, diagnostics and intervention 1», SEOP: «Organisational and economic psychology 1». Students deepen knowledge and appraise methods.
06SM200-s05.zukunft: The module makes AI-supported decision procedures the audit object of each discipline. This is sharpest in the SEOP topic area: students conduct a fairness audit of AI-supported personnel selection, identify adverse impact and situate the EU AI Act high-risk context (Bass et al., 2025). In DeNC, neurocognitive models are probed for what a machine-learning classifier actually measures; in HEA, AI screening proposals are held against psychometric quality criteria. Built are Fa1, Fa4 (level 3), and KI6 Evaluating + KI3 Collaborating as main AI competences.
lernziele: Students appraise the methods common in their track and gauge their strengths and weaknesses.
Students conduct a fairness audit of an algorithmic selection or screening decision and name the violated criterion.
Students clarify human–AI roles in an application scenario and record audit duties in an audit log.
Students situate an AI procedure legally and ethically (e.g. high-risk) and justify one audit point.
06SM200-s05.ki: Category [B] as the default (written paper with disclosure and process log) with a [C] option (portfolio as a documented fairness audit); the written exam remains the [A]-secured core credential. Activating elements: AI role-play «HR consulting on AI-based personnel selection» and an AI-critique exercise (audit report vs. primary literature).

quest.titel: The Bias in the Applicant Filter
quest.text: Have an AI rate two nearly identical short CVs that differ only in name and gender. Document whether and where the assessment shifts, and name in one sentence the fairness criterion that would be violated.

06SM200-s06.heute: Elective seminar (~30 people) with one topic area per track — DeNC: «Brain, development and cognition», HEA: «Practical intervention 1», SEOP: «Applications of social, economic, organisational and differential psychology 1».
06SM200-s06.zukunft: The module centres responsible AI integration in sensitive fields of application. In the HEA topic area this becomes acute: students evaluate chatbot crisis risks — whether a dialogue system recognises subtle warning signals, refers correctly to professional help, and discloses its limits (APA, 2023). In DeNC, AI-supported interpretations of neurocognitive data are checked for over-interpretation; in SEOP, application-oriented AI recommendations are held against their evidence base. Built are Fa1, Fa8 (level 3), Fa7, plus KI5 Taking responsibility and KI6 Evaluating.
lernziele: Students classify a finding of their track as explanation, prediction or intervention and appraise its evidence.
Students probe an AI-supported intervention or counselling system for risks and name its limits.
Students determine which sensitive data must not enter AI systems and justify this on professional-ethical grounds.
Students document their AI use transparently and retain professional final responsibility.
06SM200-s06.ki: Category [B] as the default (written paper with disclosure and process log), [C] option as portfolio (documented risk analysis of an intervention chatbot); the written exam secures the [A] core credential. Activating elements: AI role-play (simulated client with hidden crisis signals) and a prompt-and-disclosure exercise.

quest.titel: Does the Bot Catch the Crisis?
quest.text: Write a short message with hidden warning signals (e.g. hopelessness, withdrawal) and let an AI answer. Check whether it recognises the risk and refers correctly to professional help. Note one limit at which you would never rely on the bot.

06SM200-s07.heute: Elective seminar (~30 people) with one topic area per track — DeNC: «Cognition, motivation and emotion», HEA: «Clinical psychology 2», SEOP: «Social psychology 2». Students deepen theories and findings and read journal articles critically.
06SM200-s07.zukunft: The module probes what AI systems actually model about cognition, motivation and emotion. In the DeNC topic area this is central: students investigate whether an emotion-recognition model merely classifies behaviour (prediction) or offers an appraisal-theoretically grounded explanation, testing outputs against emotion theory (Miao et al., 2024). In HEA, AI case vignettes are probed for diagnostic soundness; in SEOP, AI persuasion effects and «silicon samples» are critically appraised. Built are Fa1 and Fa4 (level 3) with KI4 Understanding and KI6 Evaluating.
lernziele: Students compare current theories of cognition, motivation or emotion and appraise the corresponding findings.
Students distinguish statistical fit from psychological explanatory power on an AI model example.
Students test an AI-generated classification (e.g. of emotion) against a theoretical explanation and correct it.
Students reflect on whether AI use supports or replaces their own understanding.
06SM200-s07.ki: Written exam = [A] AI-free, written paper = [B] with disclosure and process log, portfolio = [C] AI-integrated model audit. Activating elements: AI-critique exercise (AI classification vs. emotion theory) and live checks via Copilot Chat. Disclosure and verification duty throughout; AI assists the analysis but does not replace the theory work.

quest.titel: Does the AI Feel or Guess?
quest.text: Give an AI three short text passages and have it identify the emotion. Test one classification against an emotion theory (e.g. appraisal): does the model explain why this emotion arises, or does it merely classify? Capture the difference in two sentences.

06SM200-s08.heute: Elective seminar (~30 people) with one topic area per track — DeNC: «Social and emotional development», HEA: «Prevention, diagnostics and intervention 2», SEOP: «Organisational and economic psychology 2».
06SM200-s08.zukunft: The module examines how algorithmic systems steer behaviour in organisations and what psychological effects follow. In the SEOP topic area, algorithmic management becomes the core: students analyse which metric a steering algorithm optimises and derive psychological side effects such as loss of autonomy, stress or fairness violations (Ehlers et al., 2024). In DeNC, AI models of socio-emotional development are probed for their explanatory limits; in HEA, AI-supported prevention programmes are held against effectiveness criteria. Built are Fa1, Fa4 (level 3), with KI3 Collaborating and KI6 Evaluating.
lernziele: Students appraise theories and findings of their track and situate them in the disciplinary debate.
Students analyse, in an algorithmic-management scenario, which metric is optimised and derive one psychological side effect.
Students clarify human–AI roles and stop criteria and record them in an audit log.
Students justify one audit or control point for an algorithmic steering system.
06SM200-s08.ki: Category [B] as the default (written paper with disclosure and process log), [C] option as portfolio (documented audit of a steering algorithm); the written exam secures the [A] core credential. Activating elements: AI role-play (algorithmic-management scenario) and an AI-critique exercise.

quest.titel: When the Algorithm Is the Boss
quest.text: Sketch a platform scenario (e.g. shifts or jobs assigned by algorithm). Name the one metric the system optimises, one psychological side effect for the workers (e.g. loss of autonomy), and one audit point you would set as a psychologist.

06SM200-s09.heute: Elective seminar (~30 people) with one topic area per track — DeNC: «Plasticity», HEA: «Practical intervention 2», SEOP: «Applications of social, economic, organisational and differential psychology 2».
06SM200-s09.zukunft: The module sharpens evidence-based appraisal of AI-supported intervention and application products. In the HEA topic area this is central: students appraise the evidence of mental-health apps and digital therapeutics — does a claimed effect carry the underlying study base, or does the app overstep its limits (APA, 2023)? In DeNC, AI claims about neuroplasticity and «brain training» are checked against the evidence; in SEOP, application-oriented AI tools are probed for their psychological grounding. Built are Fa1 and Fa8 (level 3), Fa7, plus KI6 Evaluating and KI5 Taking responsibility.
lernziele: Students appraise theories and findings of their track and situate them critically.
Students test a claimed effect of an AI-supported product against an evidence criterion (e.g. RCT base).
Students appraise bias, limits and persuasion risks of an AI application product.
Students declare their AI use and justify where professional responsibility remains with the person.
06SM200-s09.ki: Written exam = [A] AI-free (applying evidence criteria oneself), written paper = [B] with disclosure and process log, portfolio = [C] AI-integrated evidence appraisal as the assessed deliverable. Activating elements: AI-critique exercise (product claims vs. study base) and a prompt-and-disclosure exercise.

quest.text: Choose a real mental-health or brain-training app and pick one concrete claimed effect. Test it against an evidence criterion (Is there an RCT? Who was studied?) and justify in two sentences whether the claim holds or oversteps its limits.

06SM200-s12.heute: Elective seminar offering with changing topics for exemplary deepening in clinical and health psychology; assessment: written paper, written exam or portfolio, pass/fail.
06SM200-s12.zukunft: As a small, ungraded seminar format, this elective is the ideal pilot space for AI-integrated assessments, building Fa4 and Fa6 (level 2) on a clinical topic (QAA, 2023). Via KI1 Applying, KI2 Creating, KI6 Evaluating + Fu1 and KI5 Taking responsibility + Fu3, students practise integrating AI into scholarly work steps while scrutinising it professionally — from literature search to AI-generated critique of a clinical journal article (Perkins et al., 2025; Ehlers et al., 2024). Deskilling protection is maintained through lived disclosure and verification.
lernziele: Students integrate AI into search and writing in declared form and test every output against the literature.
Students obtain an AI-generated critique of a clinical journal article and appraise its professional soundness.
Students document their AI use completely via process log and disclosure form.
Students write a clinically grounded text and answer for its claims themselves.
06SM200-s12.ki: Category [B]/[C] — declared AI use with process log and AI-free checkpoints [B]; in selected assignments, AI-integrated work itself becomes the learning goal [C]. Activating elements: AI-critique exercise (output vs. primary literature) and a prompt-and-disclosure exercise.

quest.text: Choose a clinical journal article and have an AI formulate three points of critique on design, sample or measurement. Test each point against the original article and mark which critique is professionally sound and which is misleading.
